title: Reading and Use of English - Part 5
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.

content.title: The Paradox of Choice in the Digital Marketplace
content.subtitle: An examination of how an abundance of options affects consumer decision-making and psychological well-being.
content.text: In the mid-20th century, the average grocery store carried roughly 3,000 items. Today, a single online marketplace can offer millions of variants of the same product. On the surface, this explosion of choice appears to be the ultimate triumph of individual liberty. We are no longer restricted by local inventory or regional tastes; we can seek out the exact object that mirrors our personal preferences. [31] However, a growing body of psychological research suggests that this perceived freedom is a double-edged sword, often leading to a state of 'decisional paralysis' rather than liberation. [/31] || The problem lies in the cognitive load required to evaluate vast amounts of information. When faced with only a few options, the brain can easily perform a cost-benefit analysis. But as the number of choices increases, the effort needed to make a rational decision begins to outweigh the potential benefit of finding the 'perfect' match. [32] This leads many to opt out of the decision-making process altogether, a phenomenon evidenced by studies where consumers offered twenty-four flavors of jam were far less likely to make a purchase than those offered only six. [/32] The sheer volume of information creates a mental friction that stifles action. || Beyond the immediate struggle to choose, there is the post-decision aftermath. In an era of limited choice, if a product was mediocre, the consumer could blame the world for its lack of variety. Today, the burden of a sub-optimal choice falls squarely on the individual. [33] If there are a thousand types of sneakers available and the pair you buy is uncomfortable, the implication is that you simply failed to do enough research or made an error in judgment. [/33] This shift in responsibility has contributed to a pervasive sense of anxiety and self-blame among modern shoppers. || Furthermore, the presence of infinite alternatives inevitably diminishes the satisfaction we derive from our eventual selection. This is known as the 'opportunity cost' of choice. [34] Even when we are relatively happy with what we have bought, our minds cannot help but wander to the features of the items we rejected, leading to a lingering sense of 'what if.' [/34] This constant comparison creates a baseline of dissatisfaction that no amount of consumption can fully alleviate. We are constantly mourning the paths not taken, even as we enjoy our chosen reality. || Social media has exacerbated this issue by introducing the 'comparison of lifestyles.' Choice is no longer just about products; it is about identity. [35] We are bombarded with curated versions of other people's lives, creating a meta-marketplace where we must choose who to be, while simultaneously fearing that we are choosing the 'wrong' version of ourselves. [/35] This digital voyeurism amplifies the feeling that somewhere, someone else has made a better choice, is having a better experience, and is living a more authentic life. || [36] To mitigate these effects, some experts advocate for 'satisficing'—a term coined by Herbert Simon—which involves setting a standard for what is 'good enough' and stopping the search once that criteria is met. [/36] In a world that demands 'maximizing' and finding the absolute best, the act of settling for a high-quality, albeit non-perfect, option may be the only way to preserve our mental health. It requires a conscious rejection of the myth that more choice always leads to more happiness.

questions.question: What is the author's main point in the first paragraph?
options: A) Individual liberty is best expressed through diverse consumer habits.
B) Online marketplaces have successfully replaced traditional grocery stores.
C) The increase in product variety is a direct result of regional tastes.
D) The abundance of options may actually hinder the freedom it claims to provide.
questions.explanation: The text states that the 'perceived freedom' of having many choices is a 'double-edged sword' that leads to 'decisional paralysis' rather than liberation.

questions.question: The 'jam study' mentioned in the second paragraph illustrates that...
options: A) an excess of options can discourage people from completing a transaction.
B) the brain is unable to perform cost-benefit analyses in grocery stores.
C) rational decision-making is impossible when shopping for food.
D) consumers generally prefer high-quality products over a large variety.
questions.explanation: The study showed that people were 'far less likely to make a purchase' when offered twenty-four flavors compared to six, showing that too much choice can prevent action.

questions.question: According to the third paragraph, why does the modern consumer feel more anxiety?
options: A) Because there are no longer any mediocre products to blame.
B) Because they feel personally responsible for any unsatisfactory purchase.
C) Because they are forced to do too much research before buying anything.
D) Because products today are generally of lower quality than in the past.
questions.explanation: The text explains that if a choice is sub-optimal, the 'burden' and 'implication' is that the individual 'failed' or 'made an error,' leading to self-blame.

questions.question: What does the author mean by the 'opportunity cost' of choice?
options: A) The literal cost of returning an item that does not meet expectations.
B) The decreased enjoyment of a product due to thoughts about rejected alternatives.
C) The financial loss incurred when buying the most expensive option.
D) The time wasted comparing products instead of enjoying them.
questions.explanation: It refers to the mind 'wandering to the features of the items we rejected,' which creates a 'lingering sense of what if' and diminishes satisfaction.

questions.question: In the fifth paragraph, the author suggests that social media...
options: A) encourages users to live more authentic lives by observing others.
B) helps people find their true identity through curated content.
C) reduces the fear of making wrong choices by showing various lifestyles.
D) has turned the concept of identity into another overwhelming choice.
questions.explanation: The author notes we are in a 'meta-marketplace' where we must 'choose who to be,' fearing we are choosing the 'wrong version' of ourselves.

questions.question: The strategy of 'satisficing' is presented as a way to...
options: A) lower our standards so we never feel disappointed.
B) achieve perfection in every decision we make.
C) reject all modern technology and return to a simpler way of living.
D) prioritize psychological well-being over the pursuit of the absolute best.
questions.explanation: It involves settling for 'good enough' to 'preserve our mental health' instead of 'maximizing' and searching for the absolute best.
